children.text: Elementary Education

content.caption: “Christ the King Elementary School Students.” Photo Courtesy of Christ the King School Facebook Page.

children.text: Diana Montejano and her siblings attended Christ the King elementary school, a private Catholic school on San Antonio’s Westside. Their parents chose to send them here rather than the local public schools because of the intense discrimination built into the elementary school system in the U.S. at that time, and in San Antonio in particular. Unlike other cities in Texas which usually have one main city-wide school district, such as Houston ISD and Austin ISD, San Antonio is uniquely fragmented into 19 different districts. In the 1940s, it had 60. These school districts were mapped around neighborhoods, which as you have seen in the section “Early Life on the Westside” were mapped by race. This meant that school districts were mapped by race, too.

content.caption: “Bexar County School Districts.” Map of the school district boundaries in Bexar County, where San Antonio is located. Edgewood Independent School District can be seen on the central Westside of the city. Produced in June, 1975 by the Planning and Community Development Department. Map courtesy of the Portal to Texas History.

children.text: The Montejanos were zoned to Edgewood ISD, which at the time was extremely overcrowded and underfunded compared to other school districts in the area, with as many as 50+ students in one classroom with one teacher. The education was also discriminatory. Edgewood ISD students, who were predominantly Mexican American, did not retrieve career counseling. They were not prepared to go to college. Instead, they were urged to attend vocational school or join the military. The only guest speakers that were brought to the schools were former convicts and criminals, to “scare” the students into avoiding a life of crime.

This is why their parents sent them to Christ the King, with the hope that they would receive a better education there. Many Mexican American parents did the same thing. Private Catholic schools were a way Mexican Americans avoided discrimination and poor educational quality at public schools, and they helped many students to attend college later in life.

.
The Catholic schools were not free of all forms of discrimination however. Despite the fact that Christ the King was run entirely by a group of Mexican nuns called the Cordi-Marian Sisters, they did not allow their students to speak Spanish in school. This was by law. Texas had made a number of English-only laws beginning in the late early 1900s with increasing strictness. In 1923, the Texas State Teachers Association closed a loophole, so that private schools were also subject to English-only laws.
.

Under these policies, Spanish could not be used as a “language of instruction,” meaning that all classes such as math, science, history, and literature could only be taught in English. Spanish could be taught as an elective, but it had to be limited to a few hours a week. In the case of Christ the King and many other schools, it could not even be the Spanish that the Montejanos and other Mexican American students were familiar with. Instead it was Castillian, a completely different dialect spoken only in Spain.

Montejano strongly resisted these and other policies at Christ the King with every chance she got. She would not stop speaking Spanish, she would not attend mass or confession, and she frequently acted out at the school’s attempts to restrict her. For this, she earned a reputation as a troublemaker, a label that would follow her throughout the rest of her educational career.
Sister Augustine's Worst Sin
Diana L. Montejano
(From “Pajinas de Mi Vida”)
She had a German-matron walk,  skin as pale as snow.  Her
black heels clicked every step, accompanied by the knuckles
of her extra-large tiger's eye rosary beads.  They matched
her eyes.  And Sister Augustine took special pleasure in
raking a yardstick against the Cloroxed walls of Little
Flower High,  where my classmates and I were rightfully
intimidated by her long skinny face squeezed into a Sister
of the Holy Ghost wimple,  making the wart at the end of her
pointy nose remind us of Dorthy’s witch rather than an
enlightened bride of Christ.  We'd whisper that Jesus
certainly could've chosen a less imposing creature for His
spouse.  Worst of all,  Sister Augustine often chose me to
teach proper decorum to -- 19th century stuff:  bowing,
genuflecting,  casting humble glances down,  beating bosoms,
etc.  Divine wisdom came in uniform standards:  brown pleated
skirts,  brown beanies,  white socks,  white blouses,  brown-and-
white oxfords,  and the constant measure of two-inch-below-
the-knees hemlines.  But among her many transgressions,  one
of the most unforgiving was making me kneel with my books on
my head so I could develop grace.  When I refused to take
Holy Communion,  she sent me for counseling so I could learn
about faith.  But neither one ever came to me.  And when I
got a police escort to school -- ditching classes in a brown
uniform wasn't easy -- Sister Augustine promised to beat the
devil out of me until my back broke her beloved ruler.  None
of her persuasions seemed to inspire me -- she took to
calling me Communist.  But her very worst sin was forbidding
me to speak Spanish except in Spanish class.  She vowed to
teach me the proper way, and when she clicked into the room,
wimple,  wart,  and beads,  I would exercise proper decorum,
reverently bowing my head as Sister Augustine rolled and
pressed her strict tongue against yellowed teeth and blessed
me with her Castilian spit,  ¡Ahtí no thé dithé!
It was the closest she ever got to converting me.
Secondary Education, Beginning Writing

Montejano spent a brief stint in public school, but it did not suit her. She frequently skipped class, and after she was expelled, her parents elected to send her back to private school. For her secondary education, Montejano attended Little Flower High School, another Catholic school also on San Antonio’s Westside. At first her troublemaking continued there. The more she was punished, the more she acted out. Many of the staff had already labeled her as a traviesa, and so they stopped expecting anything else but that from her.
.

That was until she met Sister Sabina, her English teacher. Sister Sabina changed her “punishments” from scrubbing the bathrooms, walls, and floors of the school to encouraging her to read. She would meet her student in the library every day after school, sneaking her banned books. Montejano found love in the literature. Sabina encouraged her to write about what she felt as she read, and so she did.

She began writing essays, and eventually poetry. She put her life into her words, writing about her childhood, her family, and the world around her. Sabina even encouraged her to run for editor of the school newspaper. When she won, Montejano used the paper as a platform to raise her voice about the political issues plaguing her community: The Vietnam War, voting rights, and issues she took with the church. It took one teacher to turn her life around, and to help her blossom into a writer.

The World's Fair was hosted in San Antonio, Texas in 1968, the year Montejano graduated from high school. The event was organized by local leaders, including congressman Henry B. Gonzales and Mayor Walter McCallister. They hoped that it would revitalize the city’s economy, which was still struggling in the wake of the Great Depression. Part of their bid  to host the fair was marketing San Antonio as the connection point between the two Americas, hence the name “HemiFair,” a play on the words “Hemisphere” and “Fair.”

However in their claims of racial tranquility, they completely ignored the poverty and segregation on the West and East sides. Further, the building of the fair’s facilities (including the Tower of The Americas, a conference center, and a park) required claiming and clearing 47 acres of land, including a large multi-racial neighborhood which had been "condemned" as part of Urban Renewal. Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated two days before the fair opened, making tensions in the city even worse.

.
Diana Montejano, her brother David, and his friends protested the taxes that were raised on the local population, as well as the destruction of homes and the relocation of residents in order to build HemisFair ’68. The group protested Mayor McCallister, as well as the continued war on Vietnam. It was Montejano’s first foray into activism outside of school, but it would not be her last.

content.caption: Diana Montejano and her brother David Montejano, circa 1970s.
